subject: Statistics
title: Propensity Score Matching
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Propensity Score Matching.

hook.text: You can't randomize who smokes and who doesn't. But you can find non-smokers who match smokers on everything else.

buildup.text: Propensity scores estimate the probability of receiving treatment based on observed characteristics.

discovery.text: Match treated and untreated subjects with similar propensity scores. The result mimics a randomized experiment.

twist.text: It only works for observed variables. Unmeasured confounders can still bias results. It's not true randomization.

climax.text: Propensity matching is widely used in healthcare, economics, and social science where randomization is impossible.

punchline.text: Can't randomize? Match on probability instead. Not perfect, but powerful.

quiz.question: What is the main limitation of propensity score matching?
options: It can only control for observed variables — unmeasured confounders may still bias results
It requires extremely large sample sizes
It only works in medical research
